Article Title: Rahul Gandhi's protest outside PM house ‘insincere’: Sonam Wangchuk’s wife
Article Text:
Gitanjali J Angmo, activist Sonam Wangchuk’swife, called “insincere” the flash protest led by CongressMP Rahul Gandhi

NEW DELHI: Activist Sonam Wangchuk 's wife, Gitanjali J Angmo, has criticised the flash protest led by Congress neta Rahul Gandhi near PM Narendra Modi's residence on July 21 as "insincere"."The protest outside the PM's house lacked sincerity. It would have been more sincere if it had been done by joining hands at Jantar Mantar. So, the public cannot be fooled anymore. This is an awakened India," Angmo told India Today in an interview."The awakened India movement has shown us and shown every political functionary that they now have to walk the talk. They cannot gaslight or be insincere anymore. They have to be sincere, otherwise, they will be thrown out by this young generation that has understood what they have to do and what they have to stand for..."Her remarks came two days after Rahul and other senior netas staged a sit-in near the PM's residence at 7 Lok Kalyan Marg over the crackdown on student protesters during their march towards Parliament on July 20. Taking on BJP, Angmo said, "I am very ashamed to say that BJP calls itself a Hindu nationalist party. Because there is nothing about them that is Hindu, and there's nothing about them that is nationalistic," she was quoted as saying."Everybody was there for a cause. That was a demonstration of what Hinduism is. Obstruction of justice, obstruction of freedom, obstruction of protest and terming youths as anti-nationals - this is not nationalism," she said.In an X post, Angmo, who is the co-founder of Himalayan Institute of Alternatives Ladakh (HIAL), said, "As an educator, I find it deeply painful to watch the image of our students being tarnished on media... The students' protest is taking place peacefully at Jantar Mantar. If there are reports of violence elsewhere in Delhi, please do not rush to attribute them to this movement without evidence." She added, "Our children deserve to be protected, not portrayed as villains. Their voices deserve to be heard, not drowned out by narratives that serve vested interests."
Article Summary: Activist Sonam Wangchuk's wife, Gitanjali J Angmo, has criticised the flash protest led by Congress neta Rahul Gandhi near PM Narendra Modi's residence on July 21. "The protest outside the PM's house lacked sincerity. It would have been more sincere if it had been done by joining hands at Jantar Mantar," she said.

Article Title: Fast-track courts, bill to tackle leaks: PM Modi
Article Text:
PM Modi shared a late night video message

NEW DELHI: PM Modi late Thursday announced that the Union Cabinet will on Friday discuss a bill to further strengthen the anti-cheating law to ensure stricter punishment to offenders.Hours after ordering the establishment of fast-track courts, in a recorded message posted on social media at midnight, PM said that based on the discussion a bill will be moved on Monday, when the second week of the current Parliament session begins, and govt will seek its passage at the earliest."Fast-track courts and strict punishment will be discussed in the Cabinet tomorrow and it will be given final shape after the discussion," Modi said, while underlining that govt has been working on addressing the concerns related to medical entrance exams paper leak over the last two and a half months. He said that govt had so far focused on ensuring that students did not lose a year and decided to conduct exams for 22 lakh students, using "all its might", with results also declared.PM Modi's message through the unusual mode came minutes after govt replaced the education secretary amid the ongoing protests against the NEET paper leak.Over the last two days, officials said, PMO has been engaged with the education ministry over the NEET exam, retest and K Radhakrishnan committee on NTA reforms, with over two dozen queries flowing from Seva Teerth.I know a paper leak is not an ordinary incident, it is very painful for lakhs of students and their parents... The culprits have been caught and are in jail. Our first and foremost responsibility was to ensure that the NEET aspirants did not lose an academic year. We used all resources to ensure that the re-exam was held in record time and results are out and lakhs of students are also celebrating," he said in the video message on X.The PM said more needed to be done, hinting the bill wo-uld be a step in that direction."Nothing is more important than the welfare and future of our youth! Those who try to harm (it) will not be spared," he said in a post on X.
Article Summary: PM Modi shared a late night video message on social media. He said fast-track courts and strict punishment will be discussed in the Cabinet tomorrow. Modi's message came minutes after govt replaced the education secretary amid the ongoing protests against the NEET paper leak.

Article Title: From fan to critic: Dharmendra Pradhan’s 2023 post resurfaces, reveals changing equations
Article Text:
A 2023 photo of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan and Sonam Wangchuk resurfaced recently (Picture credits: X).

Three years are a long time in public life. A 2023 photo of education minister Dharmendra Pradhan with education reformer Sonam Wangchuk and his wife Gitanjali Angmo has acquired a new political life.The March 2023 post, in which Pradhan praised the couple's ideas and commitment to transforming education, resurfaced on social media Thursday as Wangchuk's indefinite hunger strike over examination irregularities entered its 26th day.Wangchuk had responded with similar warmth, thanking Pradhan for being open to innovative ideas and saying the interaction had left them "even more committed to making NEP a reality on the ground". The contrast is sharp.Wangchuk, who had expressed enthusiasm about implementing NEP, is now the most prominent face associated with CJP 's agitation seeking accountability over NEET-UG paper leak and Pradhan's exit.
Article Summary: The March 2023 post, in which Pradhan praised the couple's ideas and commitment to transforming education, resurfaced on social media Thursday. Wangchuk is now the most prominent face associated with CJP's agitation seeking accountability over NEET-UG paper leak andPradhan's exit.

Article Title: Reached out 4 times, says govt; talks at Jantar Mantar or a neutral venue, says CJP
Article Text:
Protests continue at Jantar Mantar

NEW DELHI: Govt made a fresh outreach Thursday to agitating students for talks over the issues being raised by them, emphasising that a solution to the crisis cannot be found without dialogue."I very humbly invite you on behalf of govt to come forward and hold dialogue," Union minister Jitendra Singh, who has joined govt's main emissary and health minister J P Nadda in holding talks with activist Sonam Wangchuk, said, adding the Centre has reached out to Cockroach Janta Party protesters four times since Wednesday afternoon, but has not received any response.CJP has, however, refused to hold another round of talks after meeting Nadda at his residence on July 20, alleging they ended up "wasting" four hours without any outcome. It has insisted on holding the next meeting at Jantar Mantar or at a neutral venue and not at Nadda's residence or office. However, Nadda made it clear the meeting can happen at either of the two places, shrugging off CJP's suggestion.The govt has contested CJP's version of the July 20 meeting. On Thursday, though, the Centre emphasised its keenness to continue with talks to defuse the crisis whenever the protesters want and for as much of length of time as they wish.Union minister Jitendra Singh said, "We are ready to discuss the NEET issue and all other related issues with an open mind." Appealing to protesters, he added, "You will appreciate as much as I do that without talks we cannot find a solution. Without a solution, we cannot lay out further roadmap... Govt does not stand on prestige. Please come forward so that we can find a way out in the larger interest of the youth and the nation at large. "Highlighting PM Modi's announcement of setting up a fast-track court to ensure swift punishment for those involved in paper leaks, he said it attested to govt's commitment to going to any extent to safeguard students' welfare.Education minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation is a key CJP demand, and govt has so far given little indication that it is even considering the removal of one of its key figures, who has been a member of the Union Cabinet since 2014. Govt has instead conveyed its willingness to engage in talks with protesters over all the issues being raised by them.
Article Summary: CJP has refused to hold another round of talks after meeting Nadda at his residence on July 20. It has insisted on holding the next meeting at Jantar Mantar or at a neutral venue. The govt has contested CJP's version of the July 20 meeting.

Article Title: 'UP ke ladke' to take on BJP again, but who will show 'bigger heart'? Seat-sharing test awaits Rahul, Akhilesh
Article Text:
'UP ke ladke' delivered in 2024. Can they survive the battle over 2027?

The 'UP ke ladke' proved to be a winning combination in the 2024 Lok Sabha elections.

Saharanpur MP Imran Masood has claimed that the SP gained more than the Congress from their alliance in the 2024 Lok Sabha elections.

Bihar

Haryana

Madhya Pradesh

What's happening now?

Gautam credited INDIA bloc’s 2024 gains to Congress’s leadership, arguing that only Rahul Gandhi had national stature to take on BJP.

How the SP and Congress fared when they fought separately

NEW DELHI: What does the size of one’s heart have to do with political alliances? Perhaps quite a lot when the allies are the Congress and the Samajwadi Party, and at stake is the 2027 Uttar Pradesh assembly election.The ‘UP ke ladke’ delivered in the 2024 Lok Sabha elections. The partnership between Akhilesh Yadav and Rahul Gandhi helped the INDIA bloc win 43 of Uttar Pradesh’s 80 seats, handing the BJP its biggest electoral setback in the state in a decade.Now, as the two parties prepare for seat-sharing negotiations for next year’s assembly election, leaders on both sides want the other to show a “big heart” and be more accommodating.Akhilesh made the first appeal. At the INDIA bloc’s crisis meeting on June 9, held after Mamata Banerjee’s shock defeat in West Bengal, the SP chief had a word of advice for the Congress: “Show a large heart and be accommodative.”The message was clear: the Congress should avoid competing with regional allies and recognise their dominance in states where they remain the principal opposition force.With the Congress facing criticism from allies over its aggressive approach during elections, Rahul responded with an emphatic assurance: “I will bear every single humiliation that I have to bear to knit this group together and make it succeed.What is the main issue here? I have no interest in fighting you. I would have to be a madman to suddenly get up and say I am going to fight you because you are our allies, you are our friends, you are the people we love. ”Rahul’s conciliatory message, however, has not stopped Congress leaders in Uttar Pradesh from staking an ambitious claim. They have turned the “big heart” argument around, asking the SP to offer the Congress equal status, a respectable deal and, according to some leaders, 50:50 parity.Saharanpur MP Imran Masood has argued that the SP benefited more from the alliance in 2024.Congress co-incharge for Uttar Pradesh Rajesh Tiwari made a similar argument: “The SP is keen to head the next government in the state. Hence, it should have a big heart.”The pitch grew louder on July 2 when newly appointed Uttar Pradesh Congress in-charge Rajendra Pal Gautam said the party would enter the assembly election on the principles of “equality and respect”.These remarks appear far removed from Rahul’s conciliatory intervention at the INDIA bloc meeting in June. But the posturing by Congress leaders is hardly surprising. Seat-sharing has repeatedly emerged as a point of friction between the party and its regional allies.The same tension has surfaced in several states where the Congress has attempted to balance its national ambitions with the political dominance of regional partners.In Bihar, the Congress and the RJD failed to finalise a seat-sharing agreement even after nominations opened for the first phase of the assembly elections. The lack of coordination was evident when Tejashwi Yadav filed his nomination papers without any senior Congress leader by his side.The two parties eventually reached an agreement, but the delay had already exposed divisions within the Mahagathbandhan and disrupted its preparations. Little wonder, their Mahagathbandhan was decimated by the BJP and its allies.In Haryana, several rounds of negotiations between the Congress and Arvind Kejriwal’s AAP failed to produce a seat-sharing agreement. The two parties could not agree on the number and choice of constituencies, while the bitterness of their rivalry in Delhi made a compromise even more difficult.They eventually contested separately, splitting the opposition space in several constituencies. The Congress fell short of dislodging the BJP, which returned to power for a third consecutive term.But when it comes to seat sharing with Samajwadi Party, there is already a history of bitterness between the two allies. There was an open war of words between Akhilesh Yadav and the then CM face of Congress Kamal Nath, who refused to cede any ground to the Samajwadi Party in the state. This was immediately after the opposition had joined hands at the national level under the INDIA bloc banner to fight the BJP.Despite that the Congress high command remained a mute spectator as Kamal Nath refused to budge. Kamal Nath, who was confident of victory then, announced that the INDIA bloc was only for national elections. The bitterness complicated negotiations for the 2024 Lok Sabha elections, before an intervention by senior leaders, including Priyanka Gandhi Vadra, helped rescue the partnership.Now, as the assembly elections approach, both sides have begun posturing to keep up the pressure. Congress leaders have been more vocal, citing the party’s national status, as they often do when dealing with regional allies.In fact, newly appointed Congress in-charge Rajendra Pal Gautam, who is supposed to oversee the party’s campaign in UP, has gone on to claim that the SP’s success in the 2024 Lok Sabha elections was because the Congress was projected as the senior partner.The Congress leader has also claimed that if the party is not given its due respect within the alliance, it would amount to giving the BJP a “walkover”.Interestingly, both parties are citing past results to justify their claims. The Congress points to the Samajwadi Party’s performance in the 2014 and 2019 Lok Sabha elections to argue that its spectacular showing in 2024 was the result of its alliance with the grand old party.The SP won five seats in 2014 when it contested alone and five in 2019 when it was allied with Mayawati’s BSP. However, when the SP and Congress contested together in 2024, the SP’s tally rose to 37. Congress leaders claim that this sharp increase was due to the influence of the grand old party.Now, let us analyse the Congress’s performance in Uttar Pradesh assembly elections.The Congress has been out of power in UP since 1989. Over the last four decades, its presence in the state assembly has dwindled continuously, and the party has largely been confined to the Gandhi family bastions of Rae Bareli and Amethi. In the Lok Sabha elections, the Congress won only two seats in 2014 and one in 2019, before increasing its tally to six in 2024.The Congress and SP contested the 2017 assembly elections in alliance but were decimated by the BJP. While the SP won 47 seats, the Congress managed only seven.In the 2022 elections, the Congress went solo. Led by AICC general secretary Priyanka Gandhi Vadra, the party contested 399 seats, with women accounting for 40% of its candidates. Despite this, the party performed poorly and its vote share fell to 2.33%.The Congress managed to win only two seats—Aradhana Mishra ‘Mona’ from Rampur Khas in Pratapgarh district and Virendra Chaudhary from Pharenda in Maharajganj district. It marked the party’s worst-ever assembly election performance in Uttar Pradesh.So, will the ‘UP ke do ladke’ unite again in 2027, as they did in 2024, to stun the BJP-led NDA in Uttar Pradesh? There has been considerable bonhomie on display between Rahul Gandhi and Akhilesh Yadav. The two used their birthdays, 12 days apart, to reinforce their shared commitment to fighting the BJP. But to ensure that this bonhomie translates into electoral gains for both parties, they will have to rein in their leaders, especially those in the Congress.When the Congress surprised everyone with a sit-in protest outside Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s residence in the national capital over the NEET issue, Akhilesh was by Rahul Gandhi’s side. The two leaders have also held a closed-door meeting in Parliament, along with Priyanka Gandhi, perhaps to work out the contours of a practical seat-sharing formula acceptable to both parties.The stakes are higher for the SP, which is in contention to form the next government in UP and knows that support from its allies could be crucial to achieving that objective. The Congress, with just two seats in the assembly, has little to lose in the state. However, the grand old party would do well to realise that another botched alliance experiment could isolate it further within the INDIA bloc, which it leads.The BJP in Uttar Pradesh is going through a challenging phase amid recent controversies. But chief minister Yogi Adityanath is leading the fight himself and has been quick to capitalise on the reported differences between the two allies. While Congress leaders may be making strong remarks to build pressure on Akhilesh, they should realise that this ploy carries the risk of creating a perception among voters that the two allies are fighting. And if the Congress and SP lose this perception battle, winning the election could become difficult. Bihar has already shown how damaging such an impression can be.The SP and Congress have already shown that they can hurt the BJP when they fight together. The challenge in 2027 will be to ensure that the fight over seats does not begin before the fight for Uttar Pradesh. Whether they can set aside competing ambitions and agree on who must show the 'bigger heart' will determine if the 'UP ke ladke' can deliver again.
Article Summary: The 'UP ke ladke' proved to be a winning combination in the 2024 Lok Sabha elections. The partnership between Akhilesh Yadav and Rahul Gandhi helped the INDIA bloc win 43 of Uttar Pradesh’s 80 seats. Now, as the two parties prepare for seat-sharing negotiations, leaders on both sides want the other to show a ‘big heart’ and be more accommodating.

Article Title: Tanul Thakur On The Corporate Cruelty Driving The H-1B Visa Economy
Article Text:
Tanul Thakur’s 'Wild Wild East' Uncovers the Human Cost of the American Dream

Q:

How did this book come about?

A

:

Q: I

t

was

as if your mind

was

exploding

and you had to put it out there to say,

what on earth

are we living side by side with?

A

Q: W

hat exactly is the

business

model of a desi

consultancy

?

Q: How did you persuade the protagonists to talk to you?

Q: T

his H1B

scam

is

kind of a

parallel economy which

seems to have

no closure to it.

A

Excerpts:I was an H1B worker in the US from 2011 to 2013. And during my time there as an electrical engineer, I got more than a whiff that there was some scam going on in the H1B visa programme... And this idea really stayed with me, this idea of primarily Indians exploiting Indians in the US. Cut to a few years later, I returned to India to pursue my passion of being a full-time writer and a journalist. And this idea then really thundered back to me in all its different manifestations... I think subconsciously I wanted to write a book that would explore morality, enslavement, identity of modern day Indian elites... The actual story though snowballed into a much bigger and much more significant something...This started off as a very literary project... I wanted to write a "non-fiction novel"... Then these questions started to ping my conscience: why do we not know about this scam? Who benefits from it, apart from the obvious corporate American entities? And then I figured out that this is not just about a few individuals or a few firms.It's essentially like an entire society colluding to both perpetrate and also hide the scam. .. You have these immigration forums, anonymous people with fake monikers talking about how they scam the system. You have visa mills and a bunch of parties coming together to perpetrate something really, really horrific. So the book changed during the process. I changed during the process.Sometimes it felt like a science fiction story... I was constantly taken aback, not just by the brazenness of the scam, but also about how this was hiding in plain sight... On the one hand, you are seeing all these crazy things that are happening, and on the other, there's absolute silence about it in the outer world... There were many heartbreaking things that I found... I was very surprised by the kinds of entities that I used to consider heroes. Say the American media, academia, some politicians — most of them turned out to be villains... Just understanding how it is humanly possible for human beings, in this case three protagonists, to absorb so much of pain, to absorb so much of anguish... on a day to day basis and yet have the courage to solder on. These are the things that really stayed with me.Think of it as a chain of blocks. So essentially you have a client, which is to say like a Fortune 500 firm or a corporate America entity which wants — I mean back in 2010 or something — let's say wanted a Java specialist. So it would ask a big outsourcing giant, which is called a prime vendor, do you have a Java specialist for this kind of a contractual position? If they do, they supply that. If they don't, that's when the desi consultancy model comes in, it would ask a smaller outsourcing or a staffing company. And that's how the chain gets built. You have a client, you have a prime vendor, then you have one or two companies in between. Then you have an H1B employer who is sponsoring the visa for an H1B worker. Now, a decade ago, if a client was paying around $120 per hour for a Java specialist or for an IT specialist with six years of experience, different parties would take a cut and the H1B worker would get somewhere around $25 an hour.And amidst that, there is insane amounts of wage theft. You have your purchase orders being edited, you have no idea how much your employer is getting... There is this thing called benching, wherein you're supposed to pay the H1B worker when he or she doesn't have a project. But that never happens. In fact, you don't just not get paid when you're benched. You have to pay your employers taxes during that period. There is this immense amount of psychological cruelty that's meted by Indian owners of body shops or desi consultancies to Indian H1B workers. This dehumanization exists at varying levels. There's this literal and metaphorical transformation of identity. You are often handed a resume which has seven or eight years of fake experience. Then you get a job through a proxy interview. Even at the client side, if you don't know anything, you have this process of like on job support where you can outsource your job to some other techie in India. So very rampant exploitation almost becomes the language of this industry.First of all, they were themselves so frustrated... Nobody thought their stories were stories. Nobody thought of them as people. So the very fact that I told them that I'm interested in this, that I completely understand your pain, anguish, maybe it had kind of helped that I had done some bit of my own research... I think it was just my persistence. And I think in that persistence, they had seen some bit of my wonder, my innocence, my intellectual curiosity, but also my rage, my very incipient rage. .. They all got eased into the process because I think they had understood that he's not a regular journalist, which is to say that he genuinely cares for us as people... I think being genuinely open, innocent and curious about their lives as people and applying largely the same matrices, but icing it with a copious amount of rage about the systemic abuse itself, I think kind of endeared them to talk to me.What occurred to me around 2020 is that this is a book about the tyranny of capitalism. It's about the tyranny or the dictates of American neoliberalism. And I am essentially just using H1B as a metaphor or as a lens to explore that... One can very easily and glibly dismiss a lot of American workers as racist or bigoted or xenophobic. Some of them could be, sure. But many of them are actually not just these things. They are simply very saddened about their economic and psychological and personal devastation. But that's also one of the biggest corporate cruelties, right? Or it's one of the biggest corporate tricks... A lot of Americans are just blaming Indians, which is insane to me because given the way this H1B system has been abused... they should not be each other's adversaries. They should understand that they are both being pitted against each other... This is something that I want a lot of people to understand, that this is essentially not about immigrants versus citizens, it's about immigrants and citizens versus exploitative corporations and the exploitative American capitalist structure itself.
Article Summary: Tanul Thakur was an H1B worker in the US from 2011 to 2013. He says he wanted to write a book that would explore morality, enslavement, identity of modern day Indian elites. The book changed during the process, he says.

Article Title: Mamata passes Martyrs' Day street test; marathon begins now
Article Text:
TMC chairperson Mamata Banerjee addresses supporters during Martyrs’ Day function in Kolkata.

Congress commemorating Martyrs Day

What happened on July 21?

Some martyrs family went to Mamata's event, others attended Ritabrata's.

NCPI bloc at Rajghat

Reaching for the old playbook

What happened on July 21

Taking the long route

A new alliance arithmetic?

The rebels’ narrowing road

Kalyan Banerjee addressing the crowd

Beyond the street show

On 20th July night, Mamata Banerjee refused to leave the Martyrs' Day stage after her faction alleged that it had been vandalised. Less than two weeks earlier, a protest march led by her had been disrupted by clashes with people the TMC identified as BJP workers. A veteran politician that she is, Mamata refused to take another chance and kept a night-long vigil.There was something faintly Shakespearean about the scene, a former ruler separated from the machinery of her kingdom, surrounded by a diminished circle of loyalists and guarding a stage her faction said had been vandalised.By the next afternoon, she was back on stage, speaking largely extempore. There were fewer people to thank, with the guest list missing its usual glamour quotient, and fewer formalities to observe. The poetry was not as eloquent as in the past. Her Hindi was toned down, now that Delhi was no longer calling. The plans she laid out were not grandiose; in fact, they were skeletal, reflecting a fear of further backstabbing. Yet Didi was in her element, refusing to throw in the towel without a fight.For TMC workers, Mamata remains the agni-kanya, the leader who can blaze through opposition by sheer force of action. But this July 21 was an acid test.After the May 4 defeat and the desertion of a large part of the party’s rank and file, Mamata had to prove to her detractors, and perhaps to herself, that she could still draw a crowd without the full weight of the well-oiled party machinery or the organisational muscle of many of her former lieutenants.By that limited yardstick, Mamata passed with flying colours.The breakaway faction cut a sorry figure, with a TOI report citing a paltry attendance of 500 , far below Mamata’s gathering. The Congress , which held its own Martyrs’ Day observance, surprisingly managed to cobble together a reasonable gathering. This was no longer the old Dharmatala sea. But Mamata still occupied the largest space.Martyrs’ Day commemorates 12 Youth Congress workers who were killed in police firing during a procession towards Writers’ Buildings on July 21, 1993. A 13th participant remains missing and is commemorated alongside them. The march was led by Mamata Banerjee and, significantly, the demand was for mandatory photo identity cards for voters. A commission report recently tabled in the state assembly described the firing as “worse than the Jallianwala Bagh massacre”.Former registrar of Calcutta University and professor in the Department of South and Southeast Asian Studies at the same university, Dr Raja Gopal Dhar Chakrabarti, speaking to TOI, recalls that fateful day in 1993, when he believes Mamata protested against “Leftist anarchy”. The frenzy in the crowd was maddening, and many who wanted to join the protest could not reach the venue, he reminisces.According to Chakrabarti, who has diligently tracked the day since then, July 21 has, over the years, morphed into something much bigger. Mamata completely appropriated the event even after leaving the Congress and virtually turned it into the TMC’s annual political day. It measured not merely popular support but organisational obedience. This year, ironically, July 21 became a test of who represented the real TMC.The martyrs’ families, too, remained divided, with members of some families attending the Ritabrata Banerjee-led Trinamool event and others choosing Mamata’s programme. But the loyalty of those who mattered politically appeared to rest firmly with the Kalighat camp.Speaking to TOI, political analyst and former Vidyasagar University vice-chancellor Dr.Sibaji Pratim Basu says that Mamata was never short of charisma, right from her early days. This year’s Martyrs’ Day merely reiterated that she still retains the ability to be a natural crowd-puller. Chakrabarti believes that, compared with Mamata’s heyday, this year’s July 21 was a much more tepid affair, but she undoubtedly managed to establish that support remains with her.In the assembly election, the TMC won 80 seats, polling 40.8% of the votes and falling 32.1 lakh votes short of the BJP’s overall tally of 2.92 crore. Thus, while the party received a royal thrashing, fuelled largely by anti-incumbency, its core vote bank remains robust.A lot of water has flowed down the Hooghly since May. The TMC has effectively broken into three parts, with the NCPI parliamentary bloc and the Ritabrata faction splintering from the original party. The NCPI bloc, fronted by Sudip Bandyopadhyay and Kakoli Ghosh Dastidar, has abandoned even the fig leaf of anti-BJP mobilisation that the MLAs led by Ritabrata continue to maintain in Kolkata. A large number of legislators have left Mamata’s camp, with her trusted aide Madan Mitra among the latest defectors. The precise strength of the competing factions remains contested, but the Mamata-led TMC is undoubtedly a much smaller political entity than it was immediately after the election.With future municipal and panchayat contests looming, its organisational presence could be squeezed further if Bengal’s earlier political pattern repeats itself. In that context, July 21 acquired additional importance. It was virtually a referendum in the people’s court.During her fiery speech, Mamata also doubled down on Abhishek Banerjee’s position in the party hierarchy. At the same time, she reached for tricks from her old playbook that made old-timers fondly turn back the clock by two decades.In 2004 and 2006, the TMC suffered two devastating electoral jolts. The backfiring of the “India Shining” campaign in 2004 meant that NDA ally TMC was reduced to a solitary Lok Sabha seat. In the 2006 assembly election, the party was obliterated, slumping to only 30 seats. Many had written Mamata’s political obituary. However, she rose like a phoenix, aided by some luck, the CPI(M)’s complacent governance and mounting anti-incumbency. But nothing came to her on a platter.Political observers who closely followed the TMC’s rise during that period recall how Mamata, alongside Mukul Roy, took the long and arduous route of rebuilding the organisation through small meetings in the districts. She rode pillion on a motorcycle to reach places where ordinary people had been affected. Both the Singur and Nandigram movements could have fizzled out if not for her sustained campaigns. The focus on returning to the grassroots, pun intended, was reiterated by the former chief minister this time as well.According to Basu, Mamata managed to break the perception that the CPI(M) was the party of the poor and went on to become the toast of the subalterns. But this time, the competition is more formidable. However, Basu believes that the BJP’s honeymoon period has been incredibly short, although nothing will change on the ground before the 2029 Lok Sabha polls.Chakrabarti, too, echoes similar sentiments about the mood already souring against the BJP, especially over the mass deletions from the Annapurna scheme, and believes Mamata can become a strong challenger again.That is where the real test begins. With Bengal’s history of giving incumbents a long rope, her work is cut out for her. Rebuilding the organisation across 294 constituencies is a physically taxing proposition, believes Chakrabarti.Most importantly, who will play the role of Mukul Roy, silently building the organisation and ironing out the rough edges? Roy had largely controlled the organisation during Mamata’s first term and was once described by party insiders as the leader who could run it almost single-handedly. Abhishek Banerjee’s often flashy style of politics is not for the faint-hearted. It is more T20, whereas the need is perhaps for a good old-fashioned Test-match grind.Across Bengal, the TMC lost ground amid concerns over corruption, shoddy governance and the conduct of local leaders. According to Mamata, the corrupt have now left and there is nothing to worry about. That may energise those who stayed. It is not, by itself, an adequate diagnosis of defeat. But Basu believes Mamata still largely retains, among her supporters, the image of being untouched by the stench of corruption, which works in her favour. According to Chakrabarti, Mamata can regain traction but has to be careful about using Abhishek Banerjee. The noted political commentator is of the opinion that leaders may be sceptical about rejoining the TMC if Abhishek continues to be such a vital cog in the wheel.Mamata has, for now, firmly shut the door on those who deserted the party. She also unequivocally backed Abhishek, even asking workers to contact the MP if the need arose. Madan Mitra, speaking from the opposition camp, gave Mamata one month to “leave Abhishek’s hand” if she wanted the leaders back. Mamata, though, appears to be in no mood for reconciliation. She also issued a warning to Suvendu Adhikari, suggesting that she could reveal what he had done during his years as a TMC minister.Another interesting facet of Mamata’s speech was her courting of INDIA bloc partners. This was in sharp contrast to her pre-election attitude, when the then chief minister, harbouring larger ambitions, was in no mood to give any ground to either the Congress or the Left. Circumstances have changed, and so has Mamata’s outreach. But will the Congress and the Left play ball? For the Left, the distance may be too great for it to hobnob with the party that displaced it and subsequently occupied much of its political space. For the Congress, however, the TMC remains a more natural ally.Mamata’s prominent photographs at the Congress’s Shahid Minar observance amounted to more than a subtle overture. The Congress used images of her role in the 1993 movement even while accusing the TMC of later turning Martyrs’ Day into a political instrument. It is no secret that the Congress high command has often been warmer towards Mamata than its state leadership. But with Mamata cut to size, the Congress could now seek a far more equitable arrangement in any future alliance. According to Chakrabarti, Muslims are unlikely to ever move towards the BJP. The educated among them may veer towards Naushad Siddiqui of the ISF, but the rest would choose between the TMC and the Congress. Thus, a future tie-up is very much within the realm of possibility.For the breakaway faction, July 21 was a brutal reality check. Whatever the reasons behind its desertion of the mothership, it is undeniable that most of its leaders originally won because of Mamata’s support. Their options are narrowing. Returning would require Mamata to reopen a door she has publicly bolted. Contesting independently would require them to build a political identity that does not yet exist. Moving closer to the BJP would expose the hollowness of their continuing anti-BJP position. According to Basu, very few would be trusted even by the BJP because of what he describes as their political deceit.Chakrabarti is even more blunt in his assessment, saying that Ritabrata and the members of his rebel team have no future. The political scientist remains flummoxed about what exactly may have forced the likes of Firhad Hakim to cross over. What will keep Mamata worried is that even on July 21, there was a reported public bust-up with Kalyan Banerjee, who left the stage after a brief exchange. The TMC MP denied that there had been an altercation, but the episode nevertheless fed perceptions of continuing tension within the Kalighat camp.It may have been a small disagreement over speaking time. But the message it sent out may be much graver.Most of those in the crowd appeared to have come on their own, driven by their love for Didi. Leaders such as Madan Mitra and Anubrata Mondal, who had once helped oversee the party’s district mobilisation, are now in the rival camp. But elections require ample funds and organisational heft, not merely the fervent zeal of the dedicated. That is where the TMC will encounter its next big challenge.At the meeting, there was repeated emphasis on Mamata having groomed generations of leaders and on her faith in the youth. But the ledger of the past must be reconciled before the future is planned. Beyond the usual rhetoric of the BJP stealing the election, the TMC needs honest introspection about why it was ousted from power. If the leadership knew about corruption, why did it not act? What will it do differently if the public gives it another chance?According to Raja Gopal Dhar Chakrabarti, the difference between 2006 and 2026 is that, at the time, she was the challenger and people thought highly of her potential, irrespective of the electoral outcome. Now, after three terms in power, the novelty factor has worn off, and few may be willing to give her a second chance very soon.The physical demands of the intense running around needed to rebuild the party may also be extremely taxing for Mamata, Chakrabarti believes. Sibaji Pratim Basu disagrees and cites present Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Morarji Desai as examples of leaders who managed to dominate Indian politics at a more advanced age. July 21 showed that Mamata Banerjee can still own the street. It demonstrated that she is still up for a good political fight. For one afternoon, that was enough. Political resurrection, however, will be a long, tedious path. At 71, Mamata Banerjee will have to run the marathon again. Does she have the stamina for the long haul?Time will tell.
Article Summary: Martyrs’ Day commemorates 12 Youth Congress workers who were killed in police firing during a procession towards Writers’ Buildings on July 21, 1993. The march was led by Mamata Banerjee and, significantly, the demand was for mandatory photo identity cards for voters.

Article Title: 'He beats us even in socks': Amateur players can't believe as World Cup star Michael Olise joins random New York kickabout
Article Text:
France's Michael Olise holds the ball during the World Cup round of 16 soccer match between Paraguay and France in Philadelphia, Saturday, July 4, 2026. (AP Photo/Derik Hamilton)

Socks, oversized trousers and trademark quality

Strong World Cup despite missing out on a goal

Real Madrid links continue

Bayern's Michael Olise, right, talks to Bayern's Harry Kane during the Champions League opening phase soccer match between FC Bayern Munich and Sporting CP in Munich, Germany, Tuesday, Dec. 9, 2025. (AP Photo/Matthias Schrader)

Rapid rise in Munich

Just days after helping France finish fourth at the 2026 FIFA World Cup, Bayern Munich winger Michael Olise traded football's biggest stage for a casual kickabout in New York City, leaving a group of amateur players stunned after unexpectedly joining their match.The France international had featured throughout the World Cup in North America, where Les Bleus narrowly missed out on the bronze medal after suffering a dramatic 6-4 defeat to England in the third-place play-off. Despite the disappointment, Olise appeared unwilling to stay away from football for long.Instead of immediately heading home following the tournament, the 24-year-old was spotted taking part in a recreational game organised through GoodRec, a platform that connects amateur footballers looking to play matches in cities across the world.Videos shared on social media quickly went viral, showing the Bayern star lining up alongside everyday players on a public pitch in New York.Perhaps the most unusual aspect of Olise's appearance was not that he was playing in a public game, but how he was dressed.The Bayern attacker took to the field wearing socks instead of football boots and loose, chequered trousers that repeatedly slipped beneath his feet as he ran.Even so, flashes of his quality were immediately evident.One clip captured Olise cutting in from the right onto his favoured left foot, one of his trademark moves, but an opponent managed to block the effort before it could find the far corner.For the players sharing the pitch with him, it was an experience few could have imagined.GoodRec later posted a group photograph featuring Olise standing among both teams, maintaining his typically reserved expression after the game."He beats us even in socks," the platform wrote on social media. "Thank you for coming to a GoodRec game in New York City!"The Bayern forward also posed for photographs with everyone involved, adding another memorable moment to an evening that had begun as nothing more than a routine amateur football match.Olise arrived in New York after an impressive World Cup campaign with France.Although he failed to score during the tournament, the creative winger played a crucial role in Didier Deschamps' side, registering seven assists across eight appearances. His creativity made him one of the competition's standout playmakers, even as France eventually finished fourth following their defeat to England in Miami.His performances also earned him a place in FIFA's official Dream XI for the tournament, selected through fan voting, alongside the likes of Spain captain Rodri, Lionel Messi, Kylian Mbappé, Jude Bellingham and Erling Haaland.Olise's World Cup performances have only intensified speculation surrounding his future.Reports in Germany have continued to link the French international with Real Madrid, while Sport Bild has claimed France captain Kylian Mbappé attempted during the World Cup to convince his international teammate that a move to the Santiago Bernabéu would be an attractive option.However, Bayern Munich have repeatedly insisted they have no intention of selling one of their most influential players this summer.According to reports in Germany, Real Madrid have since accepted that persuading Bayern to part with Olise during the current transfer window is highly unlikely.The French winger remains under contract with the Bundesliga champions until 2029.Since arriving from Crystal Palace in the summer of 2024, Olise has developed into one of Europe's most productive attacking players.Across all competitions last season, he produced an outstanding return of 22 goals and 31 assists in 52 appearances for Bayern, establishing himself as one of the club's most important players.His spontaneous appearance on a public pitch in New York therefore came as a surprise to many, but it also reinforced the reputation Olise has built throughout his career—a footballer who simply enjoys playing the game, whether in front of tens of thousands at a World Cup or alongside local players on a community pitch just days after the tournament had ended.
Article Summary: Bayern Munich winger Michael Olise plays on public pitch in New York City. Olise played for France at the 2026 FIFA World Cup in North America. The 24-year-old has been linked with a move to Real Madrid. Bayern have repeatedly insisted they have no intention of selling Olise.

Article Title: 'My last match was World Cup final': Argentina defender Nicolas Otamendi announces international retirement
Article Text:
Argentina's Nicolas Otamendi (19) (AP Photo/Ed Zurga)

Decorated career comes to an end

Argentina defender Nicolas Otamendi has announced his retirement from international football, ending a remarkable 17-year career with the national team.The 38-year-old leaves as one of Argentina's most successful defenders, having won the 2022 FIFA World Cup and two Copa America titles. His final appearance came in Argentina's 1-0 defeat to Spain in the FIFA World Cup final in the United States.Otamendi made 139 appearances for Argentina and was a key figure in the team's success over the years. After the final, he shared an emotional message on Instagram, thanking the country for giving him the chance to achieve his biggest dream."Today I have to write the most difficult words of my career. As fate would have it, my last match was a World Cup final. It wasn't the result we wanted, but I'm leaving with my head held high, knowing that this team gave it their all right up to the very last second. Thank you, Argentina, for allowing me to fulfil the dream of becoming a world champion," he wrote.Argentina's hopes of defending their World Cup title ended with a narrow extra-time loss to Spain in the final.The team had shown great fighting spirit throughout the tournament, recovering from two goals down to beat Egypt in the Round of 32 before staging another dramatic comeback against England in the semi-finals.However, Spain proved too strong in the final and ended Argentina's reign as world champions.Otamendi also recently returned to Argentina to join River Plate after a successful club career in Europe. During his time abroad, he played for Porto, Valencia, Manchester City and Benfica, winning several major honours.Having represented Argentina at four FIFA World Cups, Otamendi retires as one of the country's most experienced and accomplished defenders, leaving behind a legacy built on consistency, leadership and success at the highest level.
Article Summary: Nicolas Otamendi has announced his retirement from international football. The 38-year-old made 139 appearances for the Argentina national team. His final appearance came in Argentina's 1-0 defeat to Spain in the FIFA World Cup final in the United States.
