Article Title: “Three-against-one situation”: El Rubius opens up on being “targeted” in MrBeast’s viral $1M challenge
Article Text:
El Rubius reacted honestly after his elimination from MrBeast’s $1 million challenge, calling it “unfair” and claiming he was targeted in a three-against-one situation. Despite his frustration, he thanked fans and gave away his $50,000 winnings. His response felt real and balanced, especially after MrBeast’s team later sent him a cake, softening the moment.

El Rubius reflects on a tough and “unfair” exit







El Rubius did not just walk away from MrBeast’s $1 million streamer challenge. He opened up. Soon after his elimination, the Spanish creator shared a raw reaction with fans, giving them a closer look at what the experience felt like from inside the game. It was not polished or carefully framed. It felt real, and that is exactly why it caught attention so quickly.The clip and his response have since fueled conversations across the streaming space. Some viewers believe intense competitions like this naturally lead to uneven situations. Others feel Rubius may have had a point about being singled out. Either way, his words have added a human layer to a challenge that many only saw as entertainment.Speaking through an Instagram Story, Rubius did not hide his frustration, but he also made sure to thank his supporters. He said, "Guys, I just got knocked out of the game. It was a bit of an unfair elimination, but hey, at least I managed to walk away with $50,000 for you all, which I’ve already distributed. It went to the mods, the subs, and the non-subs alike."He further added, "I’m happy, of course, though admittedly, it was incredibly tough.I wasn't really a fan of the challenge itself, either. But then again... it was basically a three-against-one situation the entire time. I mean, they were targeting me specifically the whole way through. I kept trying to go after RaKai, but, well, he actually just got eliminated in the end. So, at least justice was served for Abraham. Abraham, if you’re watching this, we were all rooting for you, and standing right there with you."His message carried mixed emotions. There was disappointment, yes, but also pride in how he handled things. Giving away his $50,000 winnings to his community stood out as a gesture that meant more than the result itself.He continued with another honest note, saying, "And in the end, karma finally caught up with that little punk. Sorry, I’m being a bit toxic right now, but let's just hope the MrBeast team doesn't see this! Guys, thank you all so much. Seriously, thank you for all the support and for just being there."He added, "Every time I asked for something in the chat or whatever it was, you guys were right there. You’re incredible. I’m a little disappointed in myself, personally, mostly for not performing better, but hey, I gave it my all, and we still walked away with $50,000, so we’ve got to look on the bright side. All right, guys, big hugs to all my brothers out there!"Interestingly, the story did not end with frustration. Later that day, Rubius revealed that MrBeast’s team had sent him a cake after his exit. It was a small gesture, but it shifted the mood. It showed that even in a high-pressure challenge, there is still room for respect.In the end, Rubius’ reaction felt honest and grounded. That is what stayed with people.
Article Summary: El Rubius was eliminated from MrBeast's $1 million streamer challenge. He called it "unfair" and said he was targeted in a three-against-one situation. Despite his frustration, he thanked fans and gave away his $50,000 winnings.

Article Title: IPL craze costs techie Rs 1.46 lakh in fake RCB vs CSK ticket scam
Article Text:
Image credit: AI generated pic

RCB register their second win

Poll Vote & Share your view Do you think the demand for IPL tickets is too high every season? Yes, it's excessive. No, it's just right. 3k+ users shared opinion today 5k+ users already voted today 3k+ users shared opinion today Share Opinion

NEW DELHI: IPL comes and the demand for tickets and passes go soaring up by the fans. The desperation to get inside the stadium to watch their favourite teams in action in the IPL go high and high. When the tickets get sold out, the demand gets too hig, and the cricket fans even ask for tickets in black. But for a 25-year-old techie the craze for IPL tickets for RCB vs CSK turned too costly and he was duped for a massive Rs. 1.46 lakh.The techie, a resident of B Narayanapura, fell into a trap by a post posted by a fraudster who posed himself as a ticket seller on Instagram.As per a report in Deccan Herald, the techie alleged in a complaint to Mahadevapura Police that an individual claiming to be Sumit Biswal promised he could arrange IPL tickets along with food coupons.As per the techie, Biswal was introduced him as a senior supervisor for ticket counter at M Chinnaswamy Stadium"He asked me to come near the stadium gate number 10, saying someone would deliver the tickets within minutes, and even sent an email confirmation to gain my trust," the complainant told Deccan Herald."Initially, I agreed to buy two tickets for Rs 3,700 each,but he kept asking for more money under various pretexts such as refundable security deposits, additional ID cards and food coupons.Trusting him, I made multiple payments - even using my mother’s bank account after exhausting my own limits—and ended up transferring around Rs 1.46 lakh," he added.Bhuvneshwar Kumar’s incisive spell of 3-41, coming after a marauding batting show from Tim David, Rajat Patidar and Devdutt Padikkal, propelled Royal Challengers Bengaluru (RCB) to a commanding 43-run victory over Chennai Super Kings (CSK) in their IPL 2026 encounter at the M Chinnaswamy Stadium on Sunday.The contest saw RCB’s batting unit dismantle CSK’s attack with relentless hitting, as David’s 70 not out off just 25 balls and Patidar’s unbeaten 48 off only 19 balls provided for the late fireworks after Padikkal’s fluent 50 off 29 balls had set the tone for the daunting total.Their combined effort lifted RCB to a daunting 250/3, thus setting a new record for highest total in IPL 2026. In reply, CSK faltered under scoreboard pressure, with Sarfaraz Khan’s 50 off 24 balls and Prashant Veer’s 43 the lone acts of resistance amid a string of failures from the top order.Bhuvneshwar was at his disciplined best and even crossed the 200-mark in terms of wickets in IPL, with others also chipping in as CSK were bowled out for 207 in 19.4 overs. The comprehensive win also meant RCB have registered four consecutive triumphs over CSK for the first time in the IPL’s history.
Article Summary: Royal Challengers Bengaluru (RCB) beat Chennai Super Kings (CSK) in IPL 2026 encounter at the M Chinnaswamy Stadium on Sunday. Tim David, Rajat Patidar and Devdutt Padikkal put on a marauding batting show to lift RCB to a daunting 250/3. RCB have registered four consecutive triumphs over CSK for the first time in the IPL’s history.

Article Title: Clavicular Viral Controversy: "Trans women are.....": Clavicular’s viral moment with trans women sparks fresh conversation on internet culture
Article Text:
A viral clip featuring Clavicular and trans women is gaining attention for its calm and honest tone. The conversation explores how online language and trends move across communities. Instead of conflict, the exchange highlights shared influence and mutual awareness. Viewers are engaging with its thoughtful nature, as it reflects how internet culture evolves through collaboration rather than ownership.

Clavicular and trans women share a surprising realization about online trends







The video of Clavicular, a short-video producer, is going around the Internet, yet not the reason why. There is no yelling or over-the-top drama. It is a mere, everyday talk that seems almost novel nowadays, in the age of internet, which is overflowing with content. The scene is easy-going, the tone of the narrative is straightforward, and the characters appear more concerned with conversation than combat.The message that is being addressed is the flow of the conversation. It discusses internet culture, the way people communicate with one another and the place of trends. Rather than bringing out a debate, the encounter towards curiosity. It quietly brings attention to something that a lot of people who use the internet notice but don't often say out loud. People in different communities often have a bigger impact on each other than they think.The conversation begins with a confident and lighthearted comment from one of the trans women present. She says, "You know that trans women are like the OG lip masters, right? We are very talented in the program. Like, I do the peptides, I'm on Zeta." Her tone carries a mix of pride and humor, setting a relaxed vibe at the table.Clavicular responds in a way that keeps the mood steady but adds a different angle.He says, "That is funny because you guys were using a lot of the words in like the South Korean subreddits in like 2014." It’s not confrontational. If anything, it feels like an observation. He points to how certain words and ideas may have traveled across different online spaces over time.Another voice joins in and brings the moment full circle. "Guys, we're like the OGs. I saw a couple of posts about that. We're like copying each other." That line hits in a simple but honest way. It reflects something familiar. Online culture is rarely created in isolation. It grows through sharing, borrowing, and sometimes even unknowingly repeating.That's what makes the clip so powerful. It doesn't want to make a point or win an argument. It only shows a group of people talking, listening, and figuring something out together.This moment feels different in a space where viral content often leads to fights. It reminds people that conversations can be calm, thoughtful, and still important. And sometimes, that honest silence is what makes it work.
Article Summary: A viral clip featuring Clavicular and trans women is gaining attention for its calm and honest tone. The conversation explores how online language and trends move across communities. Viewers are engaging with its thoughtful nature, as it reflects how internet culture evolves through collaboration.

Article Title: 'Ran from one hospital to another': A mother's ordeal to save son with 'pellet' injury during Sansad Chalo march
Article Text: NEW DELHI: For nearly 12 hours, Jyoti Lochab ran from one hospital to another, pleading for someone to admit her 19-year-old son, who suffered what doctors said were “pellet-like injuries” to his right eye during the July 20 Sansad Chalo march.“He was lying there in pain while we kept being sent from one hospital to another. He kept telling me, ‘Mummy, meri aankh mein bahut dard hai.’ We folded our hands before doctors. Somehow, after spending the entire night running around, AIIMS admitted him,” she said.Delhi Police has denied allegations that pellet guns were used during the protest.Jyoti said she tried calling Sahil repeatedly that afternoon after asking him not to attend the march, but he did not answer. “We told him there could be violence and asked him not to go. But he said, ‘I’m also a student. I’ve also appeared for these exams. I should go’,” she recalled.Around 5.30 pm, she received a call from a friend of his, informing her that Sahil was admitted to Lady Hardinge Hospital. She and her brother rushed to the hospital.On Thursday, the teenager lay on a hospital bed with his right eye covered by a thick white bandage. Tiny marks were visible across his neck and upper chest.A day after undergoing surgery, Sahil recounted how a day he believed would be just another student protest changed the course of his life.“Around 3.30pm, something struck my arm and eye. My face was turned away. My arm went numb instantly. I couldn’t think properly... When I touched my eye, I felt like it was coming out. I held it in place with my hand and started running. I stopped a biker and requested him to take me to Lady Hardinge Hospital,” he said.The family’s ordeal had only begun. Jyoti said doctors at Lady Hardinge Hospital conducted preliminary examinations before asking them to shift Sahil elsewhere, saying specialised eye treatment was unavailable.The family then rushed him to two other hospitals, where Jyoti said she was told no eye specialists were available at night. They eventually turned to AIIMS where, after first being sent to the eye centre and then back to the trauma centre, Sahil was finally admitted around 4-5am the following morning.A distance-learning student, Sahil had appeared for the SSC examination last year, which was later cancelled amid allegations of irregularities, and believed students deserved justice. “I had hoped to join the police or another uniformed force. If I lose my eye, that dream is over,” he said. The family’s fears have only deepened after doctors informed them the chances of restoring vision in his right eye were slim.Originally from Haryana, the family lives in a rented accommodation in Najafgarh. Sahil is the eldest of three siblings. His mother said the family had pinned its hopes on him securing a govt job.“His father works as a driver. Doctors have told us there is less than a 1% chance of saving his eye. I only want the govt to help him with compensation and a govt job,” she said, adding that neither the govt nor CJP had reached out yet.Sahil is the fourth reported case of pellet-like injuries from the Parliament march. A doctor on duty at AIIMS Trauma Centre said Sahil’s condition was stable and he is being managed with antibiotics and pain medication, but multiple pellet-like objects are still lodged beneath his skin and further treatment would be decided by specialists.
Article Summary: Jyoti Lochab ran from one hospital to another, pleading for someone to admit her son. Sahil Lochab suffered what doctors said were “pellet-like injuries” to his right eye. Police has denied allegations that pellet guns were used during the protest. Doctors have told the family there is less than a 1% chance of saving his eye.

Article Title: Rooted in patience: How Mumbai’s bonsai lovers find stillness together
Article Text:
Mumbai's bonsai enthusiasts cultivate a quiet art form within the city. This community meets regularly to share knowledge and techniques for shaping trees. Bonsai practice teaches patience and a deep appreciation for nature's rhythms. Members find an emotional connection and therapeutic benefits in this living art. The circle ensures the survival of this unique craft through collective nurturing.

A community rooted in calm

Tiny trees, big conversations

Bonsai is the ultimate lesson in control and surrender

Passing it on, one sapling at a time





On a quiet weekday afternoon in Juhu, while Mumbai raced through traffic snarls and deadline dashes, a group of people gathered around something far more patient: bonsai trees no taller than a coffee table. Clippers in hand, stories on their lips, and soil under their nails, these bonsai lovers were tending to miniature forests and, in the process, to themselves.What began years ago as an interest in a niche of horticultural art has grown into a close-knit community of enthusiasts who meet regularly prune, wire, repot, and talk. The sessions are equal parts technique and therapy.“Bonsai teaches you to pause,” says Harsha Hinduja, a bonsai lover for the last two decades, gently adjusting the wire on a ficus. “In a city like Mumbai, we are conditioned to rush. With bonsai, you learn that growth cannot be forced, it has to be guided with care and patience. It takes eight to 10 years for a plant to begin being a bonsai. Taking care of a bonsai teaches you discipline, patience and expresses your creativity. ”The Bonsai lovers community meet on the third Thursday every month at members’ homes across the city, ensuring that the learning remains informal and hands-on.“Sometimes we get Bonsai experts from abroad like Sir Peter Chan, from England, come over to teach us the techniques and those classes go on for the whole day,” says Harsha.As the group gathers around a 12-year-old bonsai, discussions drift seamlessly from root structure to monsoon care, and from travel plans to favourite trees.Urvashi Thacker, who has been among the founding members of this bonsai community, says, “Bonsai is not about perfection. It’s about observation, empathy, and continuity. When we share knowledge, we ensure the art survives beyond us.” She adds, “A complete bonsai is a work of alive art, like a painting, but because it is a living thing, you have to keep nurturing it.”Dr Sangita Chadha, physiotherapist from Lokhandwala, who has been into this art for the last decade says, “I’ve always been passionate about gardening and bonsai is such a pleasure. These are my pets and babies. Now, I appreciate the big trees and nature’s beauty even more.”Rusi Bharucha, an engineer and artist who uses the Jin and shari techniques in bonsai says, “These techniques done with special tools and an eclectic saw creates the appearance of extreme age and natural, weathered beauty on mature bonsais that are over 35- 40 years old.” He adds, “Bonsai is the ultimate lesson in control and surrender. You shape the tree, yes, but you also have to accept its natural rhythm. That balance has changed how I look at life outside this room. ”Urvashi says,” Our’s is a community dedicated to sharing knowledge, fostering interest, and helping each other grow in this rewarding pursuit.”What sets this community apart is its eagerness to welcome newcomers. First-timers are encouraged to bring their plants, ask questions, and even make mistakes.Ami Shah, a sculpture artist, says, “I am relatively new to this bonsai community but have always found that everyone here is extremely helpful, and really encouraging. There is no gatekeeping here, no politics. Even for the mistakes I’ve made, instead of judgment, I always got guidance and help, and that keeps you coming back.”Harsha believes this openness is key to sustaining the art form. “Bonsai is not about perfection,” she says. “It’s about observation, empathy, and continuity. When we share knowledge, we ensure the art survives beyond us.”
Article Summary: Mumbai's bonsai enthusiasts cultivate a quiet art form within the city. This community meets regularly to share knowledge and techniques for shaping trees. Bonsai practice teaches patience and a deep appreciation for nature's rhythms. Members find an emotional connection and therapeutic benefits in this living art.

Article Title: Kolhapur must unite to groom new olympians inspired by Khashaba: Tejaswini Sawant
Article Text:
Kolhapur

:





A consensus emerged at a sports seminar on Thursday, held to mark the 74th anniversary of Olympian Khashaba Jadhav’s historic Olympic medal, that sports associations, the govt, industry and society must work together to produce future Olympic medallists inspired by Jadhav’s legacy.The 74th Medal Day was celebrated for the first time in Kolhapur at the Shri Datta Sports Complex under the joint aegis of the Raje Salunkhe Chalukya Rajvansh Pratishthan and the Kolhapur District and City Wrestling Association. A commemorative poster for the 75th anniversary (Amrit Mahotsav) of Jadhav’s Olympic medal was unveiled.Olympian shooter Tejaswini Sawant said, “There is no shortage of talent in Kolhapur. However, athletes should compete with themselves rather than with others. They should strive to improve themselves by one % every day. One should not give up even in the case of failure. Success comes through the values instilled by parents, honest hard work, and consistency. The love and support Kolhapur residents show for athletes is a great strength, and this energy must be passed on to the new generation. ”Former Kolhapur District Cricket Association president Bal Patankar said sports culture must become part of every household.“One does not need an official position to work in the sports sector. It is important to set aside differences between associations and ensure facilities are available to athletes. There should be effective utilization of CSR funds alongside the Central Government’s sports budget; however, sports associations must maintain transparency to achieve this,” he said.Kolhapur District and City Wrestling Association president Ravindra Patil said the city needed a state-of-the-art indoor wrestling stadium. “If substantial funds are provided for the development of talims (traditional wrestling arenas) and if wrestling receives royal-style patronage once again, a wrestler from Kolhapur could win an Olympic gold medal in the future.”Shivaji Salunkhe, president of the Raje Salunkhe Chalukya Rajvansh Pratishthan, and sports analyst and author Prof. Sanjay Dudhane also addressed the gathering.The seminar, titled “Plans and Initiatives to Produce Another Khashaba in Kolhapur,” also saw the distribution of 75 commemorative posters, designed by Prof. Dudhane, to talims and schools ahead of the 75th anniversary of Jadhav’s Olympic medal win.
Article Summary: The 74th Medal Day was celebrated for the first time in Kolhapur at the Shri Datta Sports Complex. A commemorative poster for the 75th anniversary (Amrit Mahotsav) of Jadhav’s Olympic medal was unveiled.

Article Title: 4 held for theft of 17 kg silver mask, cladding of Shivling at historic temple in Maharashtra's Khopoli
Article Text:
Khopoli police arrested four burglars for stealing silver items from a historic temple





NAVI MUMBAI: The Khopoli police in Raigad detected the burglary at the 18th century historic Shree Vireshwar temple, where 17 kg silver mask and silver cladding on Shivling was stolen on July 19 past midnight around 2 am, by arresting four accused burglars on Tuesday.The arrested accused are Amol Kasbe (36) and Vikas Agiwale (25), who were arrested from Shirur, Pune district and Dilip Jadhav (40) and Sakharam Gaikwad (40), who were arrested from Parner in Ahilyanagar district.The 17 kg silver mask and cladding on the Shivling was adorned in 2023 for which the around Rs 28 lakhs expenses were borne by the temple's trust from the donations received from devotees.Sachin Hire, senior inspector of Khopoli said, "On July 19, the assistant priest at the temple identified as Kishore Shigwan (56) lodge a complaint that the silver mask and cladding on Shivling was stolen. The thieves had entered the temple by breaking the chain and lock of the rear gate, which always remained close as the devotees were allowed entry only from the front gate.""Upon checking CCTV camera footage at the temple, it was seen that four masked men had entered the sanctum sanctorum around 2 am and decamped with the silver mask and cladding on the Shivling. Subsequently, an FIR was registered under BNS sections for house tres-pass, theft in a dwelling house and joint criminal liability. "Inspector Hire said, "Multiple teams were formed to trace the gang of burglars.Using technical expertise and informer network, we zeroed-in on four history-sheeters. They were traced to Pune and Ahilyanagar district and gradually apprehended on Tuesday.""They have revealed the place where they have hidden the stolen silver mask and cladding. Accordingly, a police team will recover the stolen valuables. Previously, there are criminal cases of theft and robbery registered against the arrested accused in Ahilyanagar and Pune districts."
Article Summary: The 17 kg silver mask and cladding on the Shivling was adorned in 2023 for which the around Rs 28 lakhs expenses were borne by the temple's trust. The arrested accused are Amol Kasbe, Vikas Agiwale, Dilip Jadhav and Sakharam Gaikwad.
